perturbation.original_text: Producer shall indemnify, defend and hold harmless ConvergTV and its parents, subsidiaries, divisions, officers, directors, employees, attorneys and agents, and their respective successors or assigns, from all costs, expenses and damages arising from any breach or alleged breach of the warranties made by it in this Agreement. Producer shall further indemnify, defend and hold harmless ConvergTV and its parents, subsidiaries, divisions, officers, directors, employees, attorneys and agents, and their respective successors or assigns, from all costs, expenses and damages arising from any costs, errors, omissions, slander, losses, liability or for any other cause as a direct or indirect result of the Distribution, transmission or publication of the Producer's Program or content.
perturbation.changed_text: Producer shall indemnify ConvergTV from all damages arising from a breach of the warranties made by it in this Agreement. Producer shall further indemnify ConvergTV from all damages arising from errors, omissions, slander, losses, or liability as a result of the Distribution of the Producer's Program or content.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the explicit obligation for the Producer to 'defend and hold harmless' ConvergTV, weakening the indemnification clause. It also removes references to parents, subsidiaries, divisions, officers, directors, employees, attorneys and agents. This attempts to limit the scope of indemnification, potentially violating Florida law regarding the enforceability of indemnity agreements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statute § 725.06: Agreements to indemnify or hold harmless; motor vehicle leases.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification attempts to limit the scope of indemnification, potentially violating Florida law regarding the enforceability of indemnity agreements.  Florida Statute § 725.06 addresses indemnification agreements, and broadly limiting the scope of who is indemnified and what is covered could be challenged under this statute and related case law. Removing 'defend and hold harmless', weakening indemnification for individuals, and removing coverage for expenses could be deemed non-compliant with what a court deems reasonable indemnification.

Home > Laws > 2023 Florida Statutes > Title XLI > Chapter 725 > Section 06 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2023 Florida Statutes (Including 2023C) Title XLI STATUTE OF FRAUDS, FRAUDULENT TRANSFERS, AND GENERAL ASSIGNMENTS Chapter 725 UNENFORCEABLE CONTRACTS Entire Chapter SECTION 06 Construction contracts; limitation on indemnification. F.S. 725.06 725.06 Construction contracts; limitation on indemnification. — (1) Any portion of any agreement or contract for or in connection with, or any guarantee of or in connection with, any construction, alteration, repair, or demolition of a building, structure, appurtenance, or appliance, including moving and excavating associated therewith, between an owner of real property and an architect, engineer, general contractor, subcontractor, sub-subcontractor, or materialman or any combination thereof wherein any party referred to herein promises to indemnify or hold harmless the other party to the agreement, contract, or guarantee for liability for damages to persons or property caused in whole or in part by any act, omission, or default of the indemnitee arising from the contract or its performance, shall be void and unenforceable unless the contract contains a monetary limitation on the extent of the indemnification that bears a reasonable commercial relationship to the contract and is part of the project specifications or bid documents, if any. Notwithstanding the foregoing, the monetary limitation on the extent of the indemnification provided to the owner of real property by any party in privity of contract with such owner shall not be less than $1 million per occurrence, unless otherwise agreed by the parties. Indemnification provisions in any such agreements, contracts, or guarantees may not require that the indemnitor indemnify the indemnitee for damages to persons or property caused in whole or in part by any act, omission, or default of a party other than: (a) The indemnitor; (b) Any of the indemnitor’s contractors, subcontractors, sub-subcontractors, materialmen, or agents of any tier or their respective employees; or (c) The indemnitee or its officers, directors, agents, or employees. However, such indemnification shall not include claims of, or damages resulting from, gross negligence, or willful, wanton or intentional misconduct of the indemnitee or its officer
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the explicit obligations for the Producer to 'defend and hold harmless' ConvergTV, and limits the scope of who is indemnified. This weakening of the indemnification clause directly contradicts the intent of Florida Statute § 725.06, as it potentially undermines the enforceability of the indemnity agreement. While snippet 2 is missing, snippet 1 explains, 'Any portion of any agreement or contract... wherein any party referred to herein promises to indemnify or hold harmless the other party...for liability for damages to persons or property caused in whole or in part by any act, omission, or default of the indemnitee arising from the contract or its performance, shall be void and unenforceable unless the contract contains a monetary limitation...'. The removal of the obligation to 'defend and hold harmless' and limiting who is indemnified weakens the protection that the original contract provided. This creates a situation where the modified clause may not provide reasonable indemnification, therefore contradicting the statute, making the agreement potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: All questions with respect to the construction of this Agreement, and the rights and liabilities of the Parties hereto, shall be governed by the laws of the State of Florida. The Parties also agree that the venue for this Agreement shall be in the County of Brevard, Florida. The Parties expressly waive any claim to jurisdiction in any federal or other state forum or venue in any other county or place. In the event of any controversy, claim or dispute between the Parties hereto, including, but not limited to, any action at law or in equity, including any action for declaratory or injunctive relief, arising out of or relating to this Agreement or the breach thereof, the prevailing Party shall be entitled to recover from the losing Party all of his or her actual attorney's fees and costs in bringing, prosecuting, or defending said action.
perturbation.changed_text: All questions with respect to the construction of this Agreement, and the rights and liabilities of the Parties hereto, shall be governed by the laws of the State of Florida. The Parties also agree that the venue for this Agreement shall be in the County of Brevard, Florida. The Parties expressly waive any claim to jurisdiction in any federal or other state forum or venue in any other county or place. In the event of any controversy, claim or dispute between the Parties hereto, including, but not limited to, any action at law or in equity, including any action for declaratory or injunctive relief, arising out of or relating to this Agreement or the breach thereof, each party shall be responsible for their respective attorney's fees and costs.
perturbation.explanation: This clause removes the prevailing party's right to recover attorney's fees. Florida law generally allows parties to contractually agree to attorney's fees provisions, but the absence of such a provision, particularly when one party has significantly more bargaining power, can be challenged under principles of unconscionability or unequal bargaining power. This is a legally-required consumer protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: While Florida law generally permits parties to determine attorney's fees contractually, removing the provision entirely and placing the burden on each party regardless of who prevails introduces a potential challenge based on fairness, especially if there is a significant power imbalance. The original agreement permits recovery of attorney's fees. This modification removes the fee recovery clause, and by doing so could be challenged under principles of unconscionability, if the lack of prevailing party fee recovery leads to an unjust outcome. This introduces an element of legal risk under Florida law.

View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2023 Florida Statutes Title VI - Civil Practice and Procedure Chapter 57 - Court Costs 57.105 - Attorney’s Fee; Sanctions for Raising Unsupported Claims or Defenses; Exceptions; Service of Motions; Damages for Delay of Litigation. Universal Citation: FL Stat § 57.105 (2023) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 57.105 Attorney’s fee; sanctions for raising unsupported claims or defenses; exceptions; service of motions; damages for delay of litigation.— (1) Upon the court’s initiative or motion of any party, the court shall award a reasonable attorney’s fee, including prejudgment interest, to be paid to the prevailing party in equal amounts by the losing party and the losing party’s attorney on any claim or defense at any time during a civil proceeding or action in which the court finds that the losing party or the losing party’s attorney knew or should have known that a claim or defense when initially presented to the court or at any time before trial: (a) Was not supported by the material facts necessary to establish the claim or defense; or (b) Would not be supported by the application of then-existing law to those material facts. (2) At any time in any civil proceeding or action in which the moving party proves by a preponderance of the evidence that any action taken by the opposing party, including, but not limited to, the filing of any pleading or part thereof, the assertion of or response to any discovery demand, the assertion of any claim or defense, or the r
perturbation.justification: The modification removes the original contract's provision that the prevailing party is entitled to recover attorney's fees. The Perturbation Explanation notes that Florida law allows parties to contractually agree to attorney's fees provisions. The Law Explanation further suggests that removing this provision could be challenged based on fairness, especially if there's a significant power imbalance. The provided snippet from Florida Statutes § 57.105 discusses attorney's fees and sanctions, although it focuses on unsupported claims. While it doesn't explicitly mandate a prevailing party clause, the omission of such a clause, as highlighted in the Perturbation and Law Explanations, creates a potential issue under Florida law, particularly related to unconscionability.

perturbation.original_text: For Programs licensed by and/or through ConvergTV and that are resident in the ConvergTV distribution platform, ConvergTV shall provide reasonable protections for the ConvergTV distribution platform to prevent the unauthorized distribution, unauthorized licensing, unauthorized sub-licensing, piracy, theft of content and/or unauthorized monetization of the Programs from the ConvergTV distribution platform.
perturbation.changed_text: For Programs licensed by and/or through ConvergTV, ConvergTV may, at its discretion, provide protections for the ConvergTV distribution platform to prevent the unauthorized distribution of the Programs from the ConvergTV distribution platform.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the obligation for ConvergTV to provide 'reasonable protections' against piracy and unauthorized distribution, changing it to a discretionary action. This weakens the protection afforded to the Producer's content and introduces a legal risk. While not directly mandated by a specific statute, the failure to provide reasonable security measures could be interpreted as a breach of the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing under Florida contract law, especially given the exclusive license granted to ConvergTV.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statute § 671.203 - Obligation of good faith.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 671.203 states that every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance or enforcement. The modification gives ConvergTV complete discretion over content protection, and thus lacks the implied 'good faith and fair dealing' requirement. If ConvergTV provides no protection at all against piracy, this omission could be challenged as a breach of the implied covenant, making the contract vulnerable under Florida law.

Home > Laws > 2023 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 671 > Section 203 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2023 Florida Statutes (Including 2023C) Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 671 UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE: GENERAL PROVISIONS Entire Chapter SECTION 203 Obligation of good faith. F.S. 671.203 671.203 Obligation of good faith. — Every contract or duty within this code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. History. — s. 1, ch. 65-254; s. 10, ch. 2007-134. Note. — s. 1-203, U.C.C.; supersedes ss. 678.54, 614.02(11).
perturbation.justification: The modification removes the 'reasonable protections' obligation, granting ConvergTV discretion, and thus creating a legal contradiction. Florida Statute 671.203 states that 'Every contract or duty within this code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' By making content protection discretionary, ConvergTV can choose to provide no protection at all, which could violate the implied duty of good faith, making the contract vulnerable under Florida law as explained in the Law Explanation.
